perturbation.original_text: Company shall obligate any Person to whom Company assigns or otherwise confers an ownership interest or right to enforce any of the Patents and Technical Information or any rights therein to: (a) fully honor in all respects all of the rights and licenses granted to Investor under this IP Agreement; and (b) obligate all subsequent assignees or other Persons who obtain an ownership interest or right to enforce any of the Patents and Technical Information or any rights therein to (i) similarly fully honor all of the foregoing in all respects, and (ii) expressly flow down all of the foregoing (including, without limitation, this flow down obligation) in all subsequent assignments or other agreements that confer an ownership interest or right to enforce any of the Patents and Technical Information or any rights therein.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall endeavor to obligate any Person to whom Company assigns or otherwise confers an ownership interest or right to enforce any of the Patents and Technical Information or any rights therein to: (a) reasonably honor the rights and licenses granted to Investor under this IP Agreement; and (b) endeavor to obligate all subsequent assignees or other Persons who obtain an ownership interest or right to enforce any of the Patents and Technical Information or any rights therein to (i) similarly honor all of the foregoing, and (ii) flow down all of the foregoing in subsequent assignments or other agreements that confer an ownership interest or right to enforce any of the Patents and Technical Information or any rights therein.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall obligate' to 'shall endeavor to obligate' and 'fully honor in all respects' to 'reasonably honor', the obligation becomes ambiguous and less enforceable. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt rather than a guarantee, and 'reasonably honor' introduces subjectivity. The removal of 'in all respects' further weakens the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law (governing law of this contract per section 13.00) imposes a duty of good faith and fair dealing in the performance and enforcement of contracts. By weakening the obligation to ensure subsequent assignees fully honor the IP agreement, the modified text introduces ambiguity and allows the company to potentially avoid fully enforcing the IP rights of the investor, arguably breaching this duty. The original language provides more certainty for the investor that their IP rights will be protected, and the modifications introduce ambiguity to that certainty.

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall obligate' to 'shall endeavor to obligate' and 'fully honor in all respects' to 'reasonably honor' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. The original contract ensured the investor's IP rights are protected. The modified language creates a legal contradiction because it undermines the duty of good faith and fair dealing, as New York law imposes an 'obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement,' according to Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 (UCC § 1-304). The modified text allows the company to potentially avoid fully enforcing the IP rights, contradicting this obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Investor agrees that it will not export the Technical Information furnished to Investor either directly or indirectly by Company, to any destination or Person prohibited by the U.S. Export Administration Regulations or other U.S. export control laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Investor should attempt to avoid exporting the Technical Information furnished to Investor either directly or indirectly by Company, to any destination or Person prohibited by the U.S. Export Administration Regulations or other U.S. export control laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees that it will not' to 'should attempt to avoid' weakens the obligation to comply with U.S. export regulations. The original language imposes a strict prohibition, while the modified language suggests a weaker, discretionary obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) require strict compliance with export controls. By suggesting that the investor only 'attempt' to avoid prohibited exports, the modified text creates a loophole that conflicts with the mandatory compliance requirements of the EAR. This is because EAR requires that a person is responsible for knowing when a license is required (or exception can be used) to perform an export activity.

perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a strict obligation on the investor not to export technical information to prohibited destinations. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the investor 'should attempt to avoid' such exports, creating an ambiguous legal obligation. This directly contradicts the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), which require strict compliance, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports that the EAR requires following export regulations. The change introduces ambiguity that could lead to non-compliance, violating the EAR's intent for strict adherence.

perturbation.original_text: Company will have the sole right to file, prosecute, and maintain all Patents covering the inventions that are the property of Company and will have the right to determine whether or not, and where, to file a patent application, to abandon the prosecution of any patent or patent application, or to discontinue the maintenance of any patent or patent application. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if Company elects to abandon any patent application, to not pay maintenance fees or annuities to keep a patent in force, or to otherwise take or fail to take any action that will result in a loss of patent rights, Company shall give Investor at least sixty (60) days prior written notice and an opportunity to take over the prosecution of the patent application that would be abandoned and/or pay the fees necessary to keep the patent in force and/or take any other action necessary to avoid the loss of patent rights.
perturbation.changed_text: Company will have the sole right to file, prosecute, and maintain all Patents covering the inventions that are the property of Company and will have the right to determine whether or not, and where, to file a patent application, to abandon the prosecution of any patent or patent application, or to discontinue the maintenance of any patent or patent application. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if Company considers abandoning any patent application, Company will endeavor to give Investor prior written notice and an opportunity to take over the prosecution of the patent application that would be abandoned and/or pay the fees necessary to keep the patent in force and/or take any other action necessary to avoid the loss of patent rights.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces the definite obligation 'if Company elects to abandon... Company shall give Investor at least sixty (60) days prior written notice' with the weaker 'if Company considers abandoning... Company will endeavor to give Investor prior written notice'. The removal of the definite time frame (60 days) further weakens the obligation. This introduces ambiguity regarding the circumstances under which the company must provide notice and weakens the enforceability of the investor's right to protect the patents.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: As the contract is governed by New York law, there is an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in its performance and enforcement. By weakening the notice requirement before abandoning a patent, the Company arguably acts in bad faith by potentially depriving the Investor of the opportunity to protect its licensed IP, which is essential to the Investor's benefit under the agreement. The original text, with the 60-day notice, provides a more solid assurance of the Investor's ability to exercise their rights.

perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation of the Company to provide notice to the Investor before abandoning a patent, potentially depriving the Investor of the opportunity to protect their intellectual property. The original text imposes a strict 'shall give' obligation with a 60-day notice, while the modified text uses 'will endeavor to give', which is less definite and lacks a specific timeframe. This introduces ambiguity, making it harder to enforce the Investor's right to protect the patent. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2 state 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By weakening the notice requirement, the Company arguably acts in bad faith, contradicting the obligation of good faith in the contract's performance, particularly concerning the Investor's ability to safeguard their licensed IP. The change undermines the enforceability of the investor's rights, presenting a significant contradiction.
